MINI MINUTES FOR FEB-01-2023 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302

LAW & MOTION, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE FEB-01-2023 HEARING RE: PETITIONER UNITED EDUCATORS OF SAN FRANCISCO'S PETITION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING. PETITIONER UNITED EDUCATORS OF SAN FRANCISCO'S PETITION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION IS GRANTED. PER CCP 1281.4, THE CASE IS STAYED PENDING ARBITRATION. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS.) ORDER SIGNED. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR.; CLERK: SEAN KANE; NOT REPORTED. (302/RBU)

calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Verified Petition To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Feb-01-2023 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MATTHEW J. MURRAY, Esq. of Altshuler Berzon LLP (Tel: 415-421-7151) for petitioner;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: PETITIONER UNITED EDUCATORS OF SAN FRANCISCO's Petition To Compel Arbitration.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Petitioner UNITED EDUCATORS OF SAN FRANCISCO's petition to compel arbitration is granted.

This case presents the issue regarding whether petitioner complied with the grievance timing requirements of the parties' Collective Bargaining Agreement ("CBA"). That procedural issue is a matter for the arbitrator to decide. (See Local Joint Exec. Bd. v. Mirage Casino-Hotel, Inc., 911 F.3d 588, 596 (9th Cir. 2018) [explaining that "timeliness, waiver, and other 'gateway' procedural matters growing out of the dispute are for [the] arbitrator."]; John Wiley & Sons, Inc. v. Livingston, 376 U.S. 543, 557 (1964) [holding that an arbitrator should decide whether the first two steps of a grievance procedure were completed, where these steps are prerequisites to arbitration]; Howsam v. Dean Witter Reynolds, Inc., 537 U.S. 79, 84-85 (2002) ["we find that the applicability of the NASD time limit rule is a matter presumptively for the arbitrator, not for the judge."].)

AT&T Technologies, Inc. v. Communication Workers of America, 475 U.S. 643 (1986) does not compel a different result. There, the Court held that the court should resolve the arbitrability issue because it involved a substantive issue, i.e., whether the parties intended to arbitrate grievances concerning layoffs predicated on a "lack of work" determination by the petitioner. This case involves the procedural issue of timing. Lastly, the court notes that Platt Pacific Inc. v. Andelson (1993) 6 Cal.4th 307 did not involve a CBA.

Per CCP 1281.4, the case is stayed pending arbitration.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)
